dupes::app/agents/[id]/page.tsx::StatCar.summary: Exact dupe cluster (3 functions, 100% similar): StatCard (app/agents/[id]/page.tsx:11), StatCard (app/agents/page.tsx:10), ...

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Catch blocks silently swallow errors in mutation handlers, leaving users with no feedback on failure

evidence: decisions/page.tsx lines 352-357: handleApprove catches errors with only console.error — no error state is set, so the UI gives no failure signal to the user after a failed approve action
decisions/page.tsx lines 363-373: handleDeny has the same pattern — error is logged but the acting state resets to false and the user sees nothing

feature-requests/page.tsx lines 202-204: handleUpdate catch block has comment 'Silently handled by polling' but polling does not surface the error either — the user's disposition change is simply dropped
detail.suggestion: Add an error state (e.g. `const [actionError, setActionError] = useState<string|null>(null)`) in each page and set it in the catch block. Render it as an inline error banner near the action buttons so the user knows the operation failed and can retry.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Pages use two different patterns for initial data loading — dedicated loadInitial+useEffect vs. direct usePolling — with no consistent rationale

evidence: app/activity/page.tsx lines 36-50: loadInitial calls fetchTrajectories() just to trigger an initial load and catch errors, then usePolling sets up its own independent fetcher for the same endpoint — two parallel fetches happen at startup
app/evolution/page.tsx lines 196-210: same pattern — loadInitial calls fetchEvolutionRecords() solely to handle the error path, while recordsPoll starts its own timer independently
app/agents/[id]/page.tsx lines 17-25: AgentDetailPage skips loadInitial entirely and relies on usePolling error state (historyPoll.error) — a different model than all other pages

app/feature-requests/page.tsx lines 181-184: FeatureRequestsPage also skips loadInitial and uses featuresPoll.error directly — consistent with agents page but inconsistent with activity/decisions/evolution
detail.suggestion: Pick one strategy codebase-wide. The cleanest is the agents/feature-requests pattern: rely on usePolling's own loading/error states and eliminate the redundant loadInitial+useEffect. This removes duplicate fetches on startup, eliminates the separate initialLoading/initialError state variables, and makes all pages consistent. Update activity, decisions, and evolution pages to match.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: All SSE event handlers swallow JSON parse errors silently, losing context about which event type failed and why

evidence: lib/api.ts lines 137-143: subscribeDesignTimeEvents catch block is empty — parse errors are invisible
lib/api.ts lines 151-159: subscribeEntityEvents same pattern
lib/api.ts lines 285-290: subscribePendingDecisions and subscribeAllPendingDecisions (lines 308-316) and subscribeEvolutionEvents (lines 373-381) all use identical empty catch blocks
A malformed event from the server will silently fail with no indication of which stream, which event type, or what the raw payload was
detail.suggestion: At minimum log the raw event data on parse failure so the issue is diagnosable: `catch (e) { console.warn('[SSE parse error]', eventType, e, (ev as MessageEvent).data?.slice(0, 200)); }`. The 200-char slice avoids dumping large payloads while giving enough context to diagnose the problem.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::m.summary: Six page-level components exceed 200 LOC with 8-12 useState hooks each, mixing data-fetching, filtering, animation, and rendering in a single function body

evidence: app/decisions/page.tsx is 620 LOC with 10 useState calls, 2 SSE subscriptions, polling setup, approval/denial handlers, CSV export logic, date-grouped history rendering, and pending-decisions card rendering all in one component
app/activity/page.tsx is 559 LOC with 12 useState calls covering: loading, error, live events, filter state, pagination, animation keys, entity selection, search query, and entity filters
app/evolution/page.tsx is 536 LOC mixing SSE subscription, three independent polling loops, sentinel check mutation, tab filtering, and five different subsection renderers inline

The SSE subscription in evolution/page.tsx (lines 213-222) references recordsPoll/insightsPoll/unmetPoll before they are declared (lines 224-239) — a temporal coupling defect that works only because of JavaScript hoisting of const declarations within the function scope but is fragile to reordering
detail.suggestion: Extract each logical section into its own component file. For decisions: DecisionsHeader, PendingDecisionsList, DecisionHistoryTable. For activity: ActivityHeader, LiveEventFeed, ActionBreakdownTable, EntityTable, FailedIntentsPanel. The page component should only compose these and wire shared state (polling results, filters). This is not about line count — it is about having one coherent responsibility per function.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::i.summary: Multiple pages use IIFE (() => { ... })() inside JSX to handle conditional logic that should be extracted into components or computed variables

evidence: app/page.tsx lines 311-337: the Spec cards section uses an IIFE to group specs by tenant and render grouped entries — this is imperative logic in the middle of JSX that obscures the render tree

app/activity/page.tsx lines 448-523: the Failed Intents section uses an IIFE spanning 75 lines that computes pagination state and renders — variables totalFailed, totalPages, reversed, and paginatedIntents are computed mid-render
detail.suggestion: Move the spec-grouping logic in page.tsx to a useMemo (e.g. `const groupedSpecs = useMemo(...)`) and create a `<SpecGroup tenant={} specs={} />` component. Move the failed intents pagination variables to useMemo/useState at the top of the component and extract a `<FailedIntentsList intents={} />` component. IIFEs in JSX are a signal that extraction is overdue.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::d.summary: The decisions page uses a single `acting` boolean to disable ALL decision cards during any single approve/deny operation, blocking unrelated decisions

evidence: app/decisions/page.tsx line 287: `const [acting, setActing] = useState(false)`
Lines 346-358 and 361-374: handleApprove and handleDeny both set acting=true globally before their await, then acting=false in finally
Lines 516-520: all DecisionCard instances receive the same acting prop — approving decision A disables decision B's buttons even though they are independent operations
detail.suggestion: Replace the global acting flag with per-decision acting state: `const [actingIds, setActingIds] = useState<Set<string>>(new Set())`. In handleApprove/handleDeny, add/remove the specific decision id. Pass `acting={actingIds.has(decision.id)}` to each DecisionCard. This allows concurrent independent decisions.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Sidebar independently polls fetchUnmetIntents on a 15-second interval while DecisionNotifierProvider already polls fetchAllDecisions every 5 seconds — uncoordinated polling from separate components watching overlapping concerns

evidence: Sidebar.tsx lines 90-101: creates its own setInterval polling fetchUnmetIntents every 15 seconds, managing its own mounted flag and cleanup — this is ad-hoc polling that duplicates the usePolling pattern used everywhere else
lib/decision-notifier.tsx lines 168-178: DecisionNotifierProvider has its own polling loop for fetchAllDecisions every 5 seconds

Both are mounted globally via app/layout.tsx. The sidebar's approach is a one-off pattern: it does not use usePolling, does not expose error state, and uses a `mounted` ref guard that the rest of the codebase does not use
detail.suggestion: Move unmet intent count to a shared context (or reuse the pattern from DecisionNotifierProvider). Create an UnmetIntentsProvider that exposes `openCount` via context, and have the Sidebar read it via `useContext`. Alternatively, lift the unmetCount polling into the existing DecisionNotifierProvider (since both are global concerns) and expose it from the same context. This eliminates the ad-hoc interval pattern in the Sidebar.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: VerificationPage manages SSE cleanup through a mutable ref rather than useEffect cleanup, creating a fragile lifecycle pattern not used anywhere else in the codebase

evidence: app/verify/[entity]/page.tsx lines 35-65: cleanupRef holds the SSE unsubscribe function. A dedicated useEffect (lines 61-65) only handles component unmount cleanup
The subscription is created inside handleRunVerification (line 112) — a user event handler — and manually cleaned up in the finally block (lines 133-137)

This means the SSE connection lifetime is managed imperatively across two scopes (event handler + useEffect) instead of being tied to React's lifecycle via a single useEffect with a dependency on the running state
All other SSE subscriptions in the codebase (activity/page.tsx, decisions/page.tsx, evolution/page.tsx) use a single useEffect that opens and closes the connection declaratively
detail.suggestion: Replace the ref-based approach with a state-driven useEffect: `useEffect(() => { if (!running) return; const cleanup = subscribeDesignTimeEvents(handleDesignTimeEvent); return cleanup; }, [running, handleDesignTimeEvent])`. This aligns with how all other pages manage SSE and removes the imperative ref/cleanup split.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: components/ directory contains two camelCase-named files amid 25 PascalCase files

evidence: The blind packet convention analysis shows components/ has 25 PascalCase names and 2 camelCase names
All React component files in the directory follow PascalCase (CascadeResults.tsx, ErrorDisplay.tsx, Sidebar.tsx, etc.) — two files deviate from this established convention
In TypeScript/React, PascalCase filenames for component files is the universal standard and matches the exported component names
detail.suggestion: Identify the two camelCase files in components/ and rename them to PascalCase. Ensure import paths in all consumers are updated.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: The majority of app pages guard polling with `enabled: !initialLoading && !initialError` but agents/[id]/page.tsx and feature-requests/page.tsx skip the guard entirely

evidence: app/agents/[id]/page.tsx line 23: `enabled: true` — polling starts immediately without any initial load guard
app/feature-requests/page.tsx lines 181-184: usePolling called with no enabled parameter (defaults to true) — polling starts unconditionally
By contrast, app/decisions/page.tsx line 324 uses `enabled: !initialLoading && !initialError`, and app/activity/page.tsx line 54 does the same

The behavioral difference is meaningful: agents and feature-requests fire their first poll immediately without waiting for any prerequisite, while decisions and activity pages coordinate startup through an initial fetch sequence
detail.suggestion: Decide which pattern is correct and apply it uniformly. If the pre-check is genuinely unnecessary for agents and feature-requests (they have no prerequisite data), document why with a comment. If the asymmetry is accidental, standardize: either all pages use a loadInitial guard or none do.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: The dashboard and feature-requests page duplicate StatCard markup inline despite StatCard being a shared component

evidence: app/page.tsx lines 285-305: renders three stat cards using raw div markup (`bg-[#111115] rounded-lg p-5`) rather than the StatCard component — the layout, font, and color classes are hand-rolled
app/feature-requests/page.tsx lines 287-310: same pattern — four hand-rolled stat cards with `bg-[#111115] rounded-lg p-3.5` instead of StatCard
components/StatCard.tsx uses `glass rounded p-3.5` as its container — a different CSS class than the dashboard's `bg-[#111115] rounded-lg p-5`, creating visual inconsistency
The blind packet also flags 'StatCard has 2 different signatures across 5 files' — confirming the component is not used uniformly
detail.suggestion: Either (a) extend StatCard to accept a `variant` prop (e.g. `glass` vs `solid`) so all pages can use the same component, or (b) accept that the dashboard's stat cards have a deliberately different treatment and document it with a comment. The current situation silently diverges across pages.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: fetchFeatureRequests returns an empty array on non-OK HTTP responses instead of throwing, unlike every other fetch function in lib/api.ts

evidence: lib/api.ts line 388: `if (!res.ok) return [];` — silently returns an empty array on any HTTP error
Every other async fetch function in the same file throws ApiError on non-OK responses (e.g. fetchSpecs line 68, fetchDecisions line 260, fetchAgents line 326)
The caller in app/feature-requests/page.tsx cannot distinguish 'server returned 500' from 'there are genuinely no feature requests' — both produce an empty list with no error display
detail.suggestion: Change fetchFeatureRequests to throw ApiError on non-OK responses, matching the rest of the module: `if (!res.ok) throw new ApiError('Failed to fetch feature requests: ' + res.status, res.status);`. The feature-requests page already has error handling via featuresPoll.error — it just never fires.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.note: Changed fetchFeatureRequests to throw ApiError on non-OK responses, matching all other fetch functions

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually replaced 'if (\!res.ok) return []' with 'throw new ApiError(...)' in fetchFeatureRequests to match the consistent error contract and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: lib/api.ts exports both async fetch functions and sync SSE subscription functions under a single module boundary with no naming signal to distinguish them

evidence: lib/api.ts exports: fetchSpecs (async), subscribeDesignTimeEvents (sync, returns cleanup), fetchEntities (async), subscribeEntityEvents (sync, returns cleanup), ... — 35 functions with two fundamentally different interaction models mixed together
The blind packet confirms `api_surface.sync_async_mix: ['lib/api.ts']`

Callers must know which category each export belongs to; there is no naming protocol (e.g. `subscribe*` vs `fetch*`) applied consistently — e.g. `checkConnection` and `triggerSentinelCheck` are async but do not start with `fetch`
detail.suggestion: The subscribe*/fetch* split is actually mostly in place — reinforce it by renaming outliers: `checkConnection` -> `fetchConnectionStatus`, `runVerification` -> `postVerification` or `triggerVerification`, `triggerSentinelCheck` -> `postSentinelCheck`. This makes the sync/async split discoverable by name without splitting the module.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: lib/decision-notifier.tsx imports directly from lib/api.ts and lib/types.ts, creating a peer-to-peer dependency within the lib/ layer rather than a clean unidirectional layering

evidence: lib/decision-notifier.tsx lines 12-13: `import { fetchAllDecisions, subscribeAllPendingDecisions } from './api'` and `import type { PendingDecision } from './types'`
lib/connection.tsx also imports from lib/api.ts (line 4)

Both connection.tsx and decision-notifier.tsx are React context providers that belong to the UI layer (they render JSX and use React hooks) but live in lib/ alongside pure data-fetching and utility modules
The result is that lib/ contains both pure utilities (api.ts, types.ts, utils.ts, hooks.ts) and React UI context providers (connection.tsx, decision-notifier.tsx) with no boundary distinction
detail.suggestion: Move connection.tsx and decision-notifier.tsx to a `providers/` directory (or `contexts/`) to signal that they are React context providers, not utility library code. lib/ should contain only framework-agnostic code (api, types, hooks, utils). This makes the dependency direction explicit: app/ and components/ import from providers/ and lib/; providers/ imports from lib/ only.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: lib/types.ts is a 469-line monolith containing unrelated domain type groups that have no coupling to each other

evidence: lib/types.ts contains at minimum 8 independent domain groups: Spec types (lines 1-40), Entity types (42-47), Verification types (49-135), Entity history types (137-171), Workflow types (193-215), Trajectory types (217-241), Evolution types (243-289), Sentinel types (291-304), WASM types (306-339), Decision types (341-369), Agent types (371-407), UnmetIntent types (409-426), FeatureRequest types (428-441)
The blind packet confirms lib/types.ts has 25 importers and a complexity score of 54, flagged for 'needs decomposition'
All 25 importing modules import from this single file regardless of which domain they operate in — a change to WasmModule has the same file as a change to SpecSummary
detail.suggestion: Split into domain-scoped type modules: `lib/types/spec.ts`, `lib/types/verification.ts`, `lib/types/evolution.ts`, `lib/types/decisions.ts`, `lib/types/agents.ts`, `lib/types/wasm.ts`, `lib/types/workflow.ts`. Maintain a `lib/types/index.ts` barrel that re-exports everything for backward compatibility. Each consumer can then import only what it needs, and changes are scoped to their domain file.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: lib/api.ts spans 8 disconnected function clusters serving different domain areas with no cohesion signal between them

evidence: lib/api.ts contains: spec functions (fetchSpecs, fetchSpecDetail), entity functions (fetchEntities, fetchEntityHistory), verification functions (runVerification, fetchVerificationStatus), SSE subscriptions (subscribeDesignTimeEvents, subscribeEntityEvents), trajectory functions (fetchTrajectories), evolution functions (fetchEvolutionRecords, fetchEvolutionInsights, fetchUnmetIntents, fetchRecordDetail, subscribeEvolutionEvents), sentinel functions (triggerSentinelCheck, triggerExtendedSentinelCheck), WASM functions (fetchWasmModules, fetchWasmInvocations), decisions functions (fetchDecisions, approveDecision, denyDecision, subscribePendingDecisions, fetchAllDecisions, subscribeAllPendingDecisions), agent functions (fetchAgents, fetchAgentHistory), feature-request functions (fetchFeatureRequests, updateFeatureRequest), and shared utilities (checkConnection, fetchMetricsText)
The blind packet confirms '8 disconnected function clusters (35 functions)' as a responsibility_cohesion flag
An evolution page and a decisions page both import from the same file, meaning a refactor of evolution API shapes can affect the decisions import graph
detail.suggestion: Split lib/api.ts by domain: `lib/api/specs.ts`, `lib/api/decisions.ts`, `lib/api/evolution.ts`, `lib/api/agents.ts`, `lib/api/wasm.ts`. Keep shared infrastructure (ApiError class, fetchWithRetry, ADMIN_HEADERS) in `lib/api/client.ts`. Maintain `lib/api.ts` as a barrel re-export during the transition. This is a large change — do it incrementally by extracting one domain cluster per PR.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: Three useEffect hooks suppress the react-hooks/exhaustive-deps rule to avoid adding poll objects to dependencies, creating stale-closure risk

evidence: app/activity/page.tsx line 69: `// eslint-disable-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` — the SSE useEffect uses trajectoryPoll.refresh() but omits trajectoryPoll from the deps array
app/decisions/page.tsx line 344: same suppression — the SSE useEffect calls decisionsPoll.refresh() without listing decisionsPoll as a dependency
app/page.tsx line 191: same pattern — SSE useEffect calls specPoll.refresh(), verifyPoll.refresh(), entityPoll.refresh() without listing the poll objects

Each case is intentionally excluding the polling hook result objects (which change identity on every render) to prevent infinite re-subscription loops. This is a design tension: the pollers need to be stable across renders for the pattern to work safely
detail.suggestion: Extract the refresh callbacks from the poll objects into stable useCallback refs before the SSE useEffect: `const refreshTrajectory = useCallback(() => trajectoryPoll.refresh(), []);` with the ref-refresh pattern (updating the ref on each render but keeping the callback identity stable). Alternatively, expose a stable `refreshRef` from usePolling itself. This eliminates the eslint suppression while keeping the behavior correct.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: app/activity/page.tsx computes pagination inside a 75-line IIFE inside JSX, making the render tree opaque and preventing unit testing of the pagination logic

evidence: app/activity/page.tsx lines 448-523: `{(() => { const totalFailed = ...; const totalPages = ...; const reversed = [...data.failed_intents].reverse(); const paginatedIntents = reversed.slice(...); return (<> ... </>); })()}` — 75 lines of imperative logic embedded in JSX
The reversed array and pagination slice happen on every render within the IIFE, making performance characteristics invisible

The pagination state variable `failedPage` and constant `FAILED_PER_PAGE` (defined at line 20 as a const inside the component, not at module scope) are used inside the IIFE — mixing state reads, computations, and rendering
detail.suggestion: Lift the pagination computation to useMemo at the component level: `const paginatedFailedIntents = useMemo(() => { const reversed = [...(data?.failed_intents ?? [])].reverse(); return { items: reversed.slice(...), totalPages: ... }; }, [data?.failed_intents, failedPage])`. Extract a `<FailedIntentsList items={} page={} totalPages={} onPageChange={} />` component. Move FAILED_PER_PAGE to module scope as a constant.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: Multiple pages call .sort() on UI data without a comparator, producing locale-dependent and potentially unstable sort order

evidence: app/activity/page.tsx line 81: `Object.keys(data.by_action).sort()` — no comparator. Object.keys order is not guaranteed to be stable across engines
app/activity/page.tsx multiple useMemo blocks extract sets and call .sort() without comparators
app/agents/[id]/page.tsx (entityTypes useMemo): `Array.from(set).sort()` — no locale-aware comparator
The blind packet flags '4x .sort() without comparator function' in activity/page.tsx and '1x .sort() without comparator function' in integrations/page.tsx and agents/page.tsx
detail.suggestion: Use explicit locale-aware comparators for user-visible sorts: `.sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b))`. For numeric sorts use explicit `(a, b) => a - b`. This is especially important for entity type names and action names which are user-visible strings.

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: updateFeatureRequest sends a PATCH request without ADMIN_HEADERS, while all other mutating decision endpoints require them

evidence: lib/api.ts lines 394-403: `fetchWithRetry(url, { method: 'PATCH', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(update) })` — no ADMIN_HEADERS included
lib/api.ts lines 267-272: approveDecision includes `...ADMIN_HEADERS` in its headers
lib/api.ts lines 277-279: denyDecision includes `headers: ADMIN_HEADERS`
lib/api.ts lines 259-261: fetchDecisions includes `headers: ADMIN_HEADERS`

ADMIN_HEADERS sets `X-Temper-Principal-Kind: admin` — its omission from updateFeatureRequest means the server may apply different authorization logic, or the call may fail in the same way SSE calls fail without admin headers (as commented at lib/api.ts line 167)
detail.suggestion: Add ADMIN_HEADERS to the updateFeatureRequest call: `headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', ...ADMIN_HEADERS }`. Review fetchFeatureRequests (line 387) as well — if feature request reads also require admin privileges, add ADMIN_HEADERS there too.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: usePolling hook accepts a wide options object with no compile-time enforcement that mutually exclusive options aren't combined

evidence: lib/hooks.ts: usePolling accepts a generic options bag with fetcher, interval, and enabled — the enabled flag is optional with no guarantee callers set it when needed
Some callers pass enabled: true or omit it entirely while others pass a complex condition — the abstraction boundary does not enforce correct usage patterns
detail.suggestion: Consider making enabled a required parameter (or providing a separate useOnceQuery hook for non-polling use cases) to make the intent explicit at each call site.

review::.::holistic::ai_generated_debt::.summary: Page components with 200-620 LOC of complex rendering logic have near-zero inline comments explaining non-obvious business decisions

evidence: app/decisions/page.tsx: 620 LOC with only 2 inline comments total — business-rule decisions like the scope-based policy preview selection (lines 415-440) have no comments explaining why each scope maps to its behavior
The comment-to-code ratio across the codebase is 0.011, suggesting comments were stripped or never added — consistent with AI-generated boilerplate that compiles but lacks intent documentation
detail.suggestion: Add comments at key decision points: why the scope mapping works the way it does, why the CSV export includes specific columns, and why the three polling intervals differ (5s, 10s, 15s). Focus on 'why' comments at non-obvious business logic, not 'what' comments on obvious code.

review::.::holistic::incomplete_migratio.summary: ErrorBoundary is the sole class component — this is appropriate since React requires class components for error boundaries, not a migration concern

evidence: components/ErrorBoundary.tsx: uses class-based React.Component — this is required by React's API for componentDidCatch and getDerivedStateFromError
All other 27 components are functional — the class component is intentional and justified
detail.suggestion: No action needed — class-based ErrorBoundary is a React requirement. The score reflects that this is properly handled.

review::.::holistic::package_organizatio.summary: lib/ directory mixes React context providers with pure utility modules, creating unclear layering

evidence: lib/connection.tsx and lib/decision-notifier.tsx are React context providers that render JSX and use React hooks
lib/api.ts, lib/utils.ts, and lib/hooks.ts are pure utilities with no JSX rendering
Both categories live in the same lib/ directory with no naming convention to distinguish them
detail.suggestion: Move connection.tsx and decision-notifier.tsx to a providers/ directory to separate React UI context from framework-agnostic utilities.

security.summary: Security coverage complete for enabled detectors.

assessment_import_audit.attest: I validated this review was completed without awareness of overall score and is unbiased.
